From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Michael Powell (director)](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell_(director)&redirect=no "Michael Powell (director)"))

English film director (1905–1990)

For other people named Michael Powell, see [Michael Powell (disambiguation)](/wiki/Michael_Powell_(disambiguation) "Michael Powell (disambiguation)").

**Michael Latham Powell** (30 September 1905 – 19 February 1990) was an English filmmaker, celebrated for his partnership with [Emeric Pressburger](/wiki/Emeric_Pressburger "Emeric Pressburger"). Through their production company [The Archers](/wiki/Powell_and_Pressburger "Powell and Pressburger"), they together wrote, produced and directed a series of classic British films, notably *[The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp](/wiki/The_Life_and_Death_of_Colonel_Blimp "The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp")* (1943), *[A Canterbury Tale](/wiki/A_Canterbury_Tale "A Canterbury Tale")* (1944), *[I Know Where I'm Going!](/wiki/I_Know_Where_I%27m_Going! "I Know Where I'm Going!")* (1945), [*A Matter of Life and Death*](/wiki/A_Matter_of_Life_and_Death_(1946_film) "A Matter of Life and Death (1946 film)") (1946, *Stairway to Heaven* in the U.S.), *[Black Narcissus](/wiki/Black_Narcissus "Black Narcissus")* (1947), [*The Red Shoes*](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)") (1948) and *[The Tales of Hoffmann](/wiki/The_Tales_of_Hoffmann_(film) "The Tales of Hoffmann (film)")* (1951).

His controversial *[Peeping Tom](/wiki/Peeping_Tom_(1960_film) "Peeping Tom (1960 film)")* (1960), which was so vilified on first release that it seriously damaged his career, is now considered a classic, and possibly the earliest "[slasher movie](/wiki/Slasher_movie "Slasher movie")".[[2]](#cite_note-Golden-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Crouse2003-5) Many renowned filmmakers, such as [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola"), [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero"), [Brian De Palma](/wiki/Brian_De_Palma "Brian De Palma"), [Bertrand Tavernier](/wiki/Bertrand_Tavernier "Bertrand Tavernier") and [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") have cited Powell as an influence.[[6]](#cite_note-Crook-6)

In 1981, Powell and Pressburger received the [BAFTA Fellowship](/wiki/BAFTA_Fellowship "BAFTA Fellowship"), the highest honour the [British Academy of Film and Television Arts](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Film_and_Television_Arts "British Academy of Film and Television Arts") can bestow upon a filmmaker. Five of their films were featured on the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute")'s list of [100 Greatest British films](/wiki/BFI_Top_100_British_films "BFI Top 100 British films").[[7]](#cite_note-BFI_(1999)-7) In 2024, their work was explored in the documentary *Made in England: The Films of Powell and Pressburger*, narrated by Scorsese.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-auto-9) [David Thomson](/wiki/David_Thomson_(film_critic) "David Thomson (film critic)") writes "There is not a British director with as many worthwhile films to his credit as Michael Powell."[[10]](#cite_note-Thomson-10)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Powell was the second son and youngest child of Thomas William Powell, a [hop farmer](/wiki/Hops "Hops"), and Mabel, daughter of Frederick Corbett, of [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England"), England. Powell was born in [Bekesbourne](/wiki/Bekesbourne "Bekesbourne"), [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), and educated at [The King's School, Canterbury](/wiki/The_King%27s_School,_Canterbury "The King's School, Canterbury") and then at [Dulwich College](/wiki/Dulwich_College "Dulwich College"). He started work at the [National Provincial Bank](/wiki/National_Provincial_Bank "National Provincial Bank") in 1922 but quickly realised he was not cut out to be a banker.

## Film career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Film career")]

Powell entered the film industry in 1925 through working with director [Rex Ingram](/wiki/Rex_Ingram_(director) "Rex Ingram (director)") at the [Victorine Studios](/wiki/Victorine_Studios "Victorine Studios") in [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice"), France (the contact with Ingram was made through Powell's father, who owned a hotel in Nice). He first started out as a general studio hand, the proverbial "gofer": sweeping the floor, making coffee, fetching and carrying. Soon he progressed to other work such as stills photography, writing titles (for the silent films) and many other jobs including a few acting roles, usually as comic characters. Powell made his film début as a "comic English tourist" in *[The Magician](/wiki/The_Magician_(1926_film) "The Magician (1926 film)")* (1926).

Returning to England in 1928, Powell worked at a diverse series of jobs for various filmmakers including as a stills photographer on [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock")'s silent film *[Champagne](/wiki/Champagne_(1928_film) "Champagne (1928 film)")* (1928). He also signed on in a similar role on Hitchcock's first "[talkie](/wiki/Talkie "Talkie")", *[Blackmail](/wiki/Blackmail_(1929_film) "Blackmail (1929 film)")* (1929). In his autobiography, Powell claims he suggested the ending in the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum") which was the first of Hitchcock's "monumental" climaxes to his films.[[11]](#cite_note-ALIM-11) Powell and Hitchcock remained friends for the remainder of Hitchcock's life.[[N 1]](#cite_note-12)

After scriptwriting on two productions, Powell entered into a partnership with American producer Jerry Jackson in 1931 to make "[quota quickies](/wiki/Cinematograph_Films_Act_1927 "Cinematograph Films Act 1927")", hour-long films needed to satisfy a legal requirement that British cinemas screen a certain quota of British films. During this period, he developed his directing skills, sometimes making up to seven films a year.[[12]](#cite_note-Powell_Early_Years-13)

Although he had taken on some directing responsibilities in other films, Powell had his first screen credit as a director on *[Two Crowded Hours](/wiki/Two_Crowded_Hours "Two Crowded Hours")* (1931). This thriller was considered a modest success at the box office despite its limited budget.[[12]](#cite_note-Powell_Early_Years-13) From 1931 to 1936, Powell was the director of 23 films, including the critically received *[Red Ensign](/wiki/Red_Ensign_(film) "Red Ensign (film)")* (1934) and *[The Phantom Light](/wiki/The_Phantom_Light "The Phantom Light")* (1935).[[12]](#cite_note-Powell_Early_Years-13)

In 1937 Powell completed his first truly personal project, *[The Edge of the World](/wiki/The_Edge_of_the_World "The Edge of the World")*. Powell gathered together a cast and crew who were willing to take part in an expedition to what was then a very isolated part of the UK. They had to stay there for quite a few months and finished up with a film which not only told the story he wanted but also captured the raw natural beauty of the location.

By 1939, Powell had been hired as a contract director by [Alexander Korda](/wiki/Alexander_Korda "Alexander Korda") on the strength of *The Edge of the World*. Korda set him to work on some projects such as *Burmese Silver* that were subsequently cancelled.[[11]](#cite_note-ALIM-11) Nonetheless, Powell was brought in to save a film that was being made as a vehicle for two of Korda's star players, [Conrad Veidt](/wiki/Conrad_Veidt "Conrad Veidt") and [Valerie Hobson](/wiki/Valerie_Hobson "Valerie Hobson"). The film was *[The Spy in Black](/wiki/The_Spy_in_Black "The Spy in Black")*, during pre-production of which Powell first met [Emeric Pressburger](/wiki/Emeric_Pressburger "Emeric Pressburger") in 1939.

### Meeting Emeric Pressburger

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Meeting Emeric Pressburger")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/P%2BP_BluePlaque.jpg/250px-P%2BP_BluePlaque.jpg)](/wiki/File:P%2BP_BluePlaque.jpg)

[English Heritage](/wiki/English_Heritage "English Heritage") [blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque") at [Dorset House](/wiki/Dorset_House,_Marylebone "Dorset House, Marylebone") in [Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone "Marylebone"), central London

The original script of *The Spy in Black* followed the book quite closely, but was too verbose and did not have a good role for either Veidt or Hobson. Korda called a meeting where he introduced a diminutive man, saying, "Well now, I have asked Emeric to read the script, and he has things to say to us."[[11]](#cite_note-ALIM-11)

Powell then went on to record (in *A Life in Movies*) how:

> Emeric produced a very small piece of rolled-up paper, and addressed the meeting. I listened spellbound. Since talkies took over the movies, I had worked with some good writers, but I had never met anything like this. In the silent days, the top [American] screenwriters were technicians rather than dramatists ... the European cinema remained highly literate and each country, conscious of its separate culture and literature, strove to outdo the other. All this was changed by the talkies. America, with its enormous wealth and enthusiasm and its technical resources, waved the big stick. ... The European film no longer existed. ... Only the great German film business was prepared to fight the American monopoly, and Dr. Goebbels soon put a stop to that in 1933. But the day that Emeric walked out of his flat, leaving the key in the door to save the storm-troopers the trouble of breaking it down, was the worst day's work that the clever doctor ever did for his country's reputation, as he was soon to find out. As I said, I listened spellbound to this small Hungarian wizard, as Emeric unfolded his notes, until they were at least six inches long. He had stood Storer Clouston's plot on its head and completely restructured the film.[[11]](#cite_note-ALIM-11)

They both soon recognised that although they were total opposites in background and personality, they had a common attitude to film-making and that they could work very well together. After making two more films together, *[Contraband](/wiki/Contraband_(1940_film) "Contraband (1940 film)")* (1940) and *[49th Parallel](/wiki/49th_Parallel_(film) "49th Parallel (film)")* (1941), with separate credits, the pair decided to form a partnership and to sign their films jointly as "Written, Produced and Directed by Michael Powell and Emeric Pressburger."[[11]](#cite_note-ALIM-11)

### The Archers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The Archers")]

Working together as co-producers, writers and directors in a partnership they dubbed "[The Archers](/wiki/Powell_and_Pressburger "Powell and Pressburger")", they made 19 feature films, many of which received critical and commercial success. Their best films are still regarded as classics of 20th-century British cinema. The [BFI 100](/wiki/BFI_Top_100_British_films "BFI Top 100 British films") list of "the favourite British films of the 20th century" contains five of Powell's films, four with Pressburger.[[13]](#cite_note-14) Thomson writes that Powell and Pressburger "struggle with great, clashing virtues—with marvelous visual imagination and uneasy, intellectual substance. [*I Know Where I'm Going*](/wiki/I_Know_Where_I%27m_Going! "I Know Where I'm Going!") is a genuinely superstitious picture; [*49th Parallel*](/wiki/49th_Parallel_(film) "49th Parallel (film)") is a strange war odyssey, with escaping Germans wandering across Canada—naïve, very violent, at times unwittingly comic, but possessed by a primitive feeling for endangered civilization; an interesting sequel is *[One of Our Aircraft is Missing](/wiki/One_of_Our_Aircraft_is_Missing "One of Our Aircraft is Missing")*—English fliers getting out of Holland; [*A Matter of Life and Death*](/wiki/A_Matter_of_Life_and_Death_(1946_film) "A Matter of Life and Death (1946 film)") is pretentious in its way, yet very funny and absolutely secure in its dainty stepping from one world to another ... [*The Thief of Bagdad*](/wiki/The_Thief_of_Bagdad_(1940_film) "The Thief of Bagdad (1940 film)") is delightful, *[The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp](/wiki/The_Life_and_Death_of_Colonel_Blimp "The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp")* a beautiful salute to Englishness ... *[Black Narcissus](/wiki/Black_Narcissus "Black Narcissus")* is that rare thing, an erotic English film about the fantasies of nuns."[[10]](#cite_note-Thomson-10) The partnership ended after *Ill Met By Moonlight*.[[14]](#cite_note-seven-15)

Although admirers would argue that Powell ought to rank alongside fellow British directors [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") and [David Lean](/wiki/David_Lean "David Lean"), his career suffered a severe reversal after the release of the controversial psychological [thriller film](/wiki/Thriller_film "Thriller film") *[Peeping Tom](/wiki/Peeping_Tom_(1960_film) "Peeping Tom (1960 film)")*, made in 1960 as a solo effort.[[15]](#cite_note-FT.com-16) The film was excoriated by mainstream British critics, who were offended by its sexual and violent images.[[2]](#cite_note-Golden-2) The film did, however, meet with the rapturous approval of the young critics of *[Positif](/wiki/Positif_(magazine) "Positif (magazine)")* and *[Midi Minuit Fantastique](/wiki/Midi_Minuit_Fantastique "Midi Minuit Fantastique")* in France, and those of *Motion* in England, and in 1965 he was subject of a major positive revaluation by [Raymond Durgnat](/wiki/Raymond_Durgnat "Raymond Durgnat") in the auteurist magazine *Movie*, later included in Durgnat's influential book *A Mirror for England*.

Powell made two films in Australia, *They're a Weird Mob* and *Age of Consent*.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-age-18)

### Zoetrope Studios

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Zoetrope Studios")]

In 1982, [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola") invited Powell to be 'senior director in residence' at his [Zoetrope Studios](/wiki/Zoetrope_Studios "Zoetrope Studios"). There, Powell "pottered around", including starting to write his autobiography. Powell's films came to have a cult reputation, broadened during the 1970s and early 1980s by a series of retrospectives and rediscoveries, as well as further articles and books. By the time of his death, he and Pressburger were recognised as one of the foremost film partnerships of all time – and cited as a key influence by many noted filmmakers such as [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and [Brian De Palma](/wiki/Brian_De_Palma "Brian De Palma").[[15]](#cite_note-FT.com-16)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/8_Melbury_Road_06.jpg/250px-8_Melbury_Road_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:8_Melbury_Road_06.jpg)

[8 Melbury Road](/wiki/8_Melbury_Road "8 Melbury Road") plaque in [Holland Park](/wiki/Holland_Park "Holland Park"), [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington "Kensington"), central London

In 1927 Powell married Gloria Mary Rouger, an American dancer; they were married in France and stayed together for only three weeks. During the 1940s, Powell had love affairs with actresses [Deborah Kerr](/wiki/Deborah_Kerr "Deborah Kerr") and [Kathleen Byron](/wiki/Kathleen_Byron "Kathleen Byron").[[11]](#cite_note-ALIM-11) From 1 July 1943 until her death on 5 July 1983, Powell was married to Frances "Frankie" May Reidy, the daughter of medical practitioner Jerome Reidy; they had two sons: Kevin Michael Powell (b. 1945) and Columba Jerome Reidy Powell (b. 1951). He also lived with actress [Pamela Brown](/wiki/Pamela_Brown_(actress) "Pamela Brown (actress)") for many years until her death from cancer in 1975.

Powell was introduced to [film editor](/wiki/Film_editing "Film editing") [Thelma Schoonmaker](/wiki/Thelma_Schoonmaker "Thelma Schoonmaker") by [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and London-based film producer Frixos Constantine.[[15]](#cite_note-FT.com-16) The couple were married from 19 May 1984 until his own death from cancer on 19 February 1990 at his home in [Avening](/wiki/Avening "Avening"), [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire").[[15]](#cite_note-FT.com-16) The couple had no children.[[18]](#cite_note-TimeOut-19)

His niece was the Australian actress [Cornelia Frances](/wiki/Cornelia_Frances "Cornelia Frances"), who appeared in bit parts in her uncle's early films.

## Preservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Preservation")]

The [Academy Film Archive](/wiki/Academy_Film_Archive "Academy Film Archive") has preserved *A Matter of Life and Death* and *The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp* by Michael Powell and [Emeric Pressburger](/wiki/Emeric_Pressburger "Emeric Pressburger").[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Powell&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legacy")]

David Thomson writes:

> I was fortunate enough to know Michael Powell in the last decade of his life. he was in America a good deal at that time: teaching for a term at [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College"); as director emeritus with [Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola")'s [American Zoetrope](/wiki/American_Zoetrope "American Zoetrope"), as treasured [Merlin](/wiki/Merlin "Merlin") in the court of [Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"); and in his marriage to the editor, [Thelma Schoonmaker](/wiki/Thelma_Schoonmaker "Thelma Schoonmaker"). I had the chance to watch many of his films with him, discussing them and learning the passion of his vision. It is all the more agreeable now to see Michael's influence spreading: the ardent antirealist has inspired so many people; the man in love with color, gesture, and cinema helped to educate viewers as well as filmmakers—not lest in the two volumes of his autobiography, *A Life in Movies* ... The great Powell and Pressburger films do not go stale; they never relinquish their wicked fun or that jaunty air of being poised on the brink. To put an arrow in our eye—to leave a nurturing wound—that was Michael's eternal thrill. I do not invoke the figure of Merlin lightly: Powell was English but Celtic, sublime yet devious, magical in the absolute certainty that imagination rules.[[10]](#cite_note-Thomson-10)

* Cited as a major influence on many film-makers such as [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"), [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola"), [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero") and [Bertrand Tavernier](/wiki/Bertrand_Tavernier "Bertrand Tavernier").[[6]](#cite_note-Crook-6) Said [Thelma Schoonmaker](/wiki/Thelma_Schoonmaker "Thelma Schoonmaker") (Scorsese's long-time film editor and Powell's third wife) of Scorsese, "Anyone he meets, or the actors he works with, he immediately starts bombarding with Powell and Pressburger movies."[[21]](#cite_note-22) Scorsese and Schoonmaker are working on restoring Powell's films, beginning with *[The Red Shoes](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)")* and *[The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp](/wiki/The_Life_and_Death_of_Colonel_Blimp "The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp")*.[[15]](#cite_note-FT.com-16)
* The **Michael Powell Award** for the Best New British Feature was instigated in 1993 at the [Edinburgh International Film Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Film_Festival "Edinburgh International Film Festival") (awarded 1993–2010 and 2012–2021). It was sponsored by the [UK Film Council](/wiki/UK_Film_Council "UK Film Council") and was "named in homage to one of Britain's most original filmmakers".[[22]](#cite_note-23)
* [Pinewood Studios](/wiki/Pinewood_Studios "Pinewood Studios"), where Powell made many of his most notable films, has named a mixing theatre in the [post-production](/wiki/Post-production "Post-production") department after him: The Powell Theatre. A giant picture of the director covers the door to the theatre, where many well-known films are mixed.
* The Film, Radio and Television Department of [Canterbury Christ Church University](/wiki/Canterbury_Christ_Church_University "Canterbury Christ Church University") has its main building named after him: The Powell Building.
* He has been played on screen by Alastair Thomson Mills in the award-winning short film *Òran na h-Eala* (2022) which explores [Moira Shearer](/wiki/Moira_Shearer "Moira Shearer")'s life changing decision to appear in *The Red Shoes*.
* A celebration entitled 'Cinema Unbound: The Creative Worlds of Powell and Pressburger' was held by the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute") in 2023, including a UK-wide programme of films and an exhibit of production and promotion materials from *The Red Shoes.*[[23]](#cite_note-24)
* Powell's work was explored in the documentary *Made in England: The Films of Powell and Pressburger* (2024), with narration by [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese").[[9]](#cite_note-auto-9)
